Line_References: Two pytest scripts for PySpark testing with different business objectives. pytest_pyspark_tests.py focuses on data transformation and aggregation testing, while ABAP_to_PySpark_Unit_Testing focuses on CSV data loading and SAP BW table operations. Both are syntactically correct and follow pytest conventions, but serve different functional purposes with moderate structural similarity.

Details: Both scripts test PySpark functionality but address fundamentally different business requirements. pytest_pyspark_tests.py tests data transformation logic (status assignment based on value > 1000 threshold) and aggregation functions, while ABAP_to_PySpark_Unit_Testing tests CSV file processing and SAP BW table loading with data validation. The test scenarios overlap conceptually (happy path, empty data, null values, error handling) but implement different business logic and data processing workflows.

Both scripts follow standard pytest conventions with @pytest.fixture decorators for SparkSession management and similar test function naming patterns. pytest_pyspark_tests.py uses 5 test cases (TC001-TC005) with direct DataFrame creation, while ABAP_to_PySpark_Unit_Testing uses 4 test cases (TC1-TC4) with CSV file creation utilities. Both implement helper functions but with different approaches - pytest_pyspark_tests.py has transform_gl_data() and aggregate_total_value() functions, while ABAP_to_PySpark_Unit_Testing has create_csv_file() and run_main_with_csv() utilities.

Script is syntactically correct with proper Python imports, valid pytest decorators, correct PySpark DataFrame operations, and well-formed function definitions. All variable references are properly defined and function calls are valid.

Script is syntactically correct with proper Python imports, valid pytest decorators, correct file I/O operations, and well-formed function definitions. All variable references are properly defined and monkeypatching operations are correctly implemented.

Both scripts demonstrate excellent syntax-level correctness with no syntax errors, undefined variables, or broken references. Both follow Python and pytest best practices with proper exception handling and resource management.

Line_References: Consider adding more comprehensive error handling tests and edge cases for the transformation logic. The current test suite covers basic scenarios well but could benefit from testing additional boundary conditions and data type variations.

Consider simplifying the monkeypatching approach and adding more granular assertions to verify specific data transformations. The current approach of redirecting stdout/stderr could be replaced with more direct testing of the underlying functions.

Both scripts would benefit from parameterized tests to reduce code duplication and improve maintainability. Consider using pytest.mark.parametrize for testing multiple data scenarios within single test functions.
